contents: Effectiveness of topical skin products in the treatmentand prevention of incontinence-associated dermatitis:a systematic reviewPriscilla Pather1  Sonia Hines1  Kate Kynoch1  Fiona Coyer2,31Nursing Research Centre and The Queensland Centre for Evidence-Based Nursing and Midwifery: a Joanna Briggs Institute Centre of Excellence,Mater Health Services, Brisbane, Australia, 2School of Nursing, Queensland University of Technology (QUT), Brisbane, Australia, and 3Intensive CareServices, Royal Brisbane and Women's Hospital, Brisbane, AustraliaE X E C U T I V E S U M M A R YBackgroundIncontinence-associated dermatitis (IAD) is inflammation of the skin resulting from repeated contact with urine and/or feces. It causes pain, redness, swelling and excoriation, and may lead to complications such as fungalskin infections and pressure injuries. It is important to prevent and treat IAD to avoid complications and improvepatient outcomes. A number of products are available for protecting skin, but evidence on their effectivenessis limited.ObjectivesThe current review aimed to establish the effectiveness of topical skin products in reducing the occurrence and/orseverity of IAD.Inclusion criteriaTypes of participantsAdult patients over the age of 18 years with incontinence and/or diarrhea.Types of interventionTopical skin products as individual interventions or part of a skin care regimen.

Types of studiesBoth published and unpublished study designs including randomized controlled trials, non-randomized controlledtrials, quasi-experimental, before and after, prospective and retrospective cohort, case-control, analytical cross-sectional, descriptive study designs including case series, individual case reports and descriptive cross-sectionalstudies across all care settings for inclusion.OutcomesThe primary outcome of interest was the absence or non-development, reduction or resolution, new development orincrease in the occurrence of IAD or the increase in severity of IAD. The secondary outcome was any adverse effectcaused by the skin care products used.Search strategyA three-step search strategy to find both published and unpublished papers was utilized in this review. Studies werelimited to those published in English from 1980 to 2016.Methodological qualityPapers selected were assessed by two independent reviewers using the Joanna Briggs Institute Meta-Analysis ofStatistics Assessment and Review Instrument (JBI-MAStARI).Correspondence: Priscilla Pather, FC declares an ongoing educational consultancy with 3MTM. PP, SH and KK have no conflicts of interest to disclose in this project.SYSTEMATICREVIEWData extractionData were extracted using the standardized data extraction tool in JBI-MAStARI. The data extracted included specificdetails about the interventions, populations, study methods and outcomes.

Data synthesisStudies were assessed for methodological quality and statistical significance to determine validity and general-izability of study results. It was not possible to pool studies to conduct meta-analysis or test for heterogeneity.ResultsThere were a limited number of clinical trials that compared products for efficacy in preventing and treating IAD.Assessment tools and severity scores used to measure skin damage outcomes were dissimilar and prone tosubjectivity. It was difficult to ascertain superiority of any individual product.ConclusionInformation on barrier protective efficacy, side effects and cost can be valuable to both clinicians and care providers.More randomized controlled trials on product effectiveness for prevention and treatment of IAD are highlyrecommended.Keywords Incontinence-associated dermatitis; nappy/napkin rash; perineal dermatitis; rash; treatmentJBI Database System Rev Implement Rep 2017; 15(5):1473-1496.

BackgroundIncontinence-associated dermatitis (IAD) is aninflammation of the skin that occurs when theskin comes into prolonged contact with urine and/orfecal matter.1 It is categorized as an inflammatoryskin condition that presents with redness, swelling,excoriation and pain, and may be complicated byopportunistic fungal infections.2 The inflammationis usually confined to skin areas that come intocontact with urine and/or feces and can includethe perianal and gluteal cleft, the buttocks, upperthighs, external genitalia and suprapubic areas.3 Itdiffers from pressure injuries in that it is ''top down''damage where superficial damage to the epidermisoccurs.4 Pressure injury on the other hand is a''bottom up'' injury where deeper tissues becomeaffected first by decreased blood flow and thenprogresses to the superficial tissues and the skin.4Incontinence and the problems associated with it inboth pediatric and adult populations have been anarea requiring significant resources in terms of nurs-ing hours and costs, and continues to be a dailychallenge for healthcare professionals in all settings.5Among the numerous difficulties associated withincontinence, skin complications are a prioritybecause of the pain, skin infections, sepsis and pres-sure injuries that can result.2,6-12The natural acidic pH of the skin ranges between4 and 6.8 with a mean of 5.5. This pH range isreferred to the as the ''protective acid mantle''.13

Prolonged exposure to moisture, urine or feces(particularly, liquid fecal matter) causes the skinpH to become more alkaline.12 This alters the skin'sacid mantle, making it more permeable to irritantsand vulnerable to bacteria and fungi.12 Skin with animpaired barrier function and abnormal trans-epidermal water loss becomes more permeable,erythematous, taut and painful, and is easily abradedand excoriated in the presence of mechanical forces,for example, shearing associated with cleansing orfriction.11 This results in a red or erythematous rashwith or without excoriation of skin that can beclassified as mild, moderate or severe IAD.14It is difficult to gauge the true scale of the problemas IAD data on both prevalence and incidencevary widely.1,20 In instances where IAD data arereported, the prevalence is estimated at 5.6-50%andincidenceat3.4-25%.1,6,15,20,21Thereisoften confusion between these two measures withinaccurate reporting and a lack of coordination ofepidemiological studies among all care settings, bothlocal and global. Most researchers focusing on IADrecommend further studies with solid researchdesigns and IAD-specific diagnostic tools to attainmore reliable data.2,5,16-18The causative factor for IAD is incontinencewhere IAD risk is increased with the type of incon-tinence (fecal incontinence, urinary incontinence ordouble incontinence [fecal and urinary] and the(c)2017 Joanna Briggs Institute. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited.frequency of episodes of incontinence [especiallyfecal] and diarrhea). Risk factors for IAD develop-ment include the use of occlusive containment prod-ucts, compromised skin (e.g. due to aging, steroid useordiabetes),immobility,diminishedcognitive

awareness, inability to perform personal hygiene,raised body temperature (pyrexia), medications(antibiotics and immunosuppressants), poor nutri-tional status and critical illness.6,19,20Prevention and treatment of IAD is focused oneffective continence clean-up or removing bodilyfluids, minimizing the length of exposure to fecalmatter, redirecting the fecal matter away from theskin and applying skin protection or some form ofbarrier.11 Studies have been carried out using anumber of products that range from petrolatum,zinc oxide, acrylate polymer and dimethicone.11Currently, practices in some clinical settingsappear to be based on availability of products, andthere is confusion and a lack of evidence that supportsthe efficacy of one product over another.5,21 As newproducts emerge on the market, it can become moredifficult to make choices as cost, marketing andcommercialization may drive selection of one productover another.22 Despite increasing recognition andefforts to manage the dermatological issues and com-plications of incontinence, to date there is a lack ofrigorously performed research addressing the effec-tiveness of different skin care products to prevent andtreat IAD.5,21The identification of a product or type of productthat best mimics the skin's barrier function, with apH close to that of intact healthy skin and thatremains on the skin as a waterproof protectant, isfundamental to reducing the development of IAD.23Products vary in formula and application method.24

They may be packaged as a liquid, emulsion, cream,spray, foam, gel, towelette, wipe or washcloth.10Products that remain on the skin, as a waterproofbarrier, isolate and protect the skin from enzymes inurine and feces and that do not rub off onto bed linenand clothes appear to be most effective.12Limited literature reviews on comparison of effec-tiveness of products were retrieved during initialsearching. Two reviews that focused on IAD treat-ment and prevention products were retrieved.22,24These studies are literature reviews, and althoughthey provide insight into studies carried out on IADtreatment and prevention, they lack the robust,comprehensive, systematic analysis and testing forheterogeneity used by a systematic review design.Cost analysis studies on different products have alsobeen carried out, and although they provide someevaluation on products and are valuable for financialcomparisons, they do not inform comprehensivelyon the efficacy of the products.These studies have recognized that much moreresearch and experimental studies are needed toidentify the products that have the best results inpreventing or treating IAD, making a systematicreview of current literature a logical starting point.An initial search of the Cochrane Library, the JBIDatabase of Systematic Reviews and Implementa-tion Reports and MEDLINE identified no othersystematic reviews on this topic.

The methods of this review were pre-specifiedaccording to a priori published protocol.25ObjectivesThe current review aimed to answer the followingquestion: is the use of topical skin products inpatients with incontinence effective in reducing theoccurrence of IAD or the risk of developing IAD inadult patients with fecal and/or urinary incontinenceand/or diarrhea and if so which products are themost effective?The quantitative objective for this review was toidentify the effectiveness of the use of topical skinproducts on: i) preventing and reducing the risk ofdeveloping IAD; and ii) reducing the occurrence andseverity of IAD in adult patients experiencing incon-tinence and/or diarrhea.More specifically, the objective was to identify theefficacy of individual topical skin products on theIAD incident rate in adult patients who are incon-tinent or have diarrhea by comparing individual skincare products with either other skin care products orwith a control group.

Inclusion criteriaTypes of participantsThe current review included studies conductedacross all settings (acute care, intensive care, agedcare, palliative care and community care) thatincluded adult patients (both male and female) overthe age of 18 years with urine incontinence, fecalincontinence with or without diarrhea, both urineand fecal incontinence with or without diarrhea, anddiarrhea by any causal factor. For the purpose of thisstudy, incontinence was defined as ''the involuntarySYSTEMATIC REVIEWP. Pather et al.(c)2017 Joanna Briggs Institute. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited.or inappropriate passing of urine and/or feces thathas an impact on social functioning or hygie-ne''.26(p.7) Diarrhea was defined using the BristolStool Form Scale.27 Both incontinence and diarrheawere acknowledged to be present as identified by theresearchers in the articles reviewed.As there are no standardized diagnostic toolscurrently used to diagnose IAD, patients who haddeveloped redness, inflammation, swelling, blister-ing or excoriation of skin in contact with urine and/or feces (as identified by the researchers in literaturereviewed) were considered as having IAD. Thisincluded the skin in the perineal, buttocks, genital,suprapubic and upper thigh regions.The review included studies on the use of topicalskin care products or barrier creams to prevent IADin patients who did not have IAD but were con-sidered at risk of developing IAD (experiencingincontinence and/or diarrhea) and skin care productsused to treat IAD in patients who were diagnosedwith or considered to have IAD as per the criteriastipulated.

The review excluded studies on individuals underthe age of 18 years, in line with the definition of''adult'' and in view of the pathophysiology andetiology of pediatric or infant nappy rash beingdifferent to adult IAD.2Types of interventionsThe current review considered clinical studies thatused topical skin products for IAD and consideredwhich was most effective based on the following:The application method of skin products inliquid, emulsion, cream, spray, foam, gel, towe-lette, wipe or washcloth forms, and compared theeffectiveness of each application method to otherapplication methods.The active ingredients in the commonly used skinproducts, such as petrolatum, zinc oxide, acrylatepolymer and dimethicone, were evaluated, andthe effectiveness of each product was comparedto the effectiveness of active ingredients inother products.The frequency of the applications, for example,once daily or ''application after each incontinentepisode'', was reviewed to establish which fre-quency method was most effective.Care bundles or skin care regimens with or with-out the use of a skin care product were analyzedfor effectiveness and compared to determinemore or less effectiveness.

Types of studiesThe review considered both published and unpub-lished experimental study designs including random-ized controlled trials, non-randomized controlledtrials, quasi-experimental studies, before and afterstudies, prospective and retrospective cohort studies,case-control studies and analytical cross-sectionalstudies for inclusion. The review also considereddescriptive study designs including case series, indi-vidual case reports and descriptive cross-sectionalstudies for inclusion.OutcomesThe current review considered studies that includedthe following primary outcome measures associatedwith skin care products used to treat or prevent IAD:The absence or non-development of IAD (red-ness, inflammation, swelling, blistering or excor-iation of skin in contact with urine and/or feces inthe perineal, buttock, genital, suprapubic andupper thigh regions).A reduction or the resolution of IAD.New development of or increase in the occur-rence of IAD.An increase in the severity of IAD symptoms.The number of days from commencement ofapplication of skin protectants to resolution ofIAD symptoms for products used that demon-strated a restorative effect on skin diagnosedwith IAD.Secondary outcome measures of interest were:

Adverse effects caused by or associated withtopical skin care products used to treat or preventthe development of IAD.Search strategyThe search strategy aimed to find both published andunpublished studies. A three-step search strategywas utilized in this review. An initial limited searchof MEDLINE (via EBSCOHost) was undertakenfollowed by an analysis of the text words containedin the title and abstract, and of the index terms usedto describe the article. A second search using allidentified keywords and index terms using BooleanOperators was then undertaken on February 19,2015 by one reviewer (Appendix I) and updatedSYSTEMATIC REVIEWP. Pather et al.(c)2017 Joanna Briggs Institute. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited.in 2016, applying the same search strategy. Third,the reference lists of all identified reports andarticlesweresearchedforadditionalstudies.Studies published in English were considered forinclusion in this review, and studies in languagesother than English were excluded as there were noresources available for translation. Studies pub-lished from 1980 to 2016 were considered forinclusion in this review as this appears to be theperiod when initial literature on the subject of IADwas published.ThedatabasessearchedincludedCINAHL,

Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials,Embase, EBSCOHost, MEDLINE (via EBSCO-Host), ProQuest Health and Medical Completeand Web of Science. The search for unpublishedstudies included Open Gray, Science.gov, Thesisand Dissertations, Clinical Trials.gov and Inter-national Standard Randomized Controlled TrialNumber Registry.Initial keywords used were: incontinence-associ-ated dermatitis, perineal dermatitis, incontinencedermatitis, perineal rash, nappy/napkin rash andtreatment. Following completion of the searches,titles and abstracts were screened by two reviewersindependently and articles for retrieval identified.Retrieved articles that met the inclusion criteria werethen subject to the critical appraisal process.The full search strategy is provided in Appendix I.Method of the reviewQuantitative papers selected for retrieval wereassessed by two independent reviewers for methodo-logical validity prior to inclusion in the review usingstandardized critical appraisal instruments from theJoanna Briggs Institute Meta-Analysis of StatisticsAssessment and Review Instrument (JBI-MAStARI)(AppendixII).Anydisagreementsthatarosebetween the reviewers were resolved through dis-cussion; a third reviewer, although available, wasnot required to resolve disparities.Data extraction

Quantitativedata wereextracted from papersincluded in the review by two reviewers using thestandardized data extraction tool from JBI-MAS-tARI (Appendix III). The data extracted includedspecific details about the interventions, populations,study methods and outcomes of significance to thereview question and specific objectives.Data synthesisStatistical pooling was not possible. Heterogeneitybetween the studies was very high because compa-rators in the studies were varied and outcomes thatmeasured skin damage severity scores were differentand prone to subjectivity. In some instances, pressureinjury scoring tools were used, making the findingsunreliable as redness due to pressure and IAD dif-fered in their location, appearance, pathophysiologyand cause. The findings have thus been presented innarrative form including tables and figures to aid indata presentation where appropriate.ResultsDescription of studiesThe searches resulted in 2193 articles being retrievedthrough systematic database searches and 13 studiesselected from reference list searches of the includedarticles. After duplicates were removed, the titles ofthe remaining 1658 articles were screened and fol-lowing exclusion by title and abstract, 228 remained.These were filtered for animal studies, magazinearticlesandpediatricstudies,and53articles

remained for full-text screening. Study verificationfor suitability was carried out by two reviewers onthese 53 articles. Twenty studies met the criteriaand were critically appraised. Following criticalappraisal, 10 studies were excluded (AppendixIV). Reasons for exclusion were outside inclusiondate, not population of interest, methodology notresearch, laboratory studies with healthy volunteersusing synthetic urine on forearms, study protocolswith no results, not in English, not intervention orcondition being reviewed and unclear measurementof IAD. Ten studies met the criteria for inclusion andwere included in the review7,15,19,28-34 (see AppendixV and Figure 1).Of the 10 studies included in this review, fivefocused on both cost effectiveness and clinicaleffectiveness with the others focusing solely onclinicalbenefits.Atotalof804participantsacross all studies were included in the final review.Of these included studies, three were randomizedcontrolledtrials,15,28,31fivequasi-experimentalstudies,7,19,29,30,34 one prospective cohort descrip-tive study32 and one prospective descriptive two-group study.33 One study was unpublished but pre-sented as a poster.29 Of these, one study comparedthe effectiveness of zinc oxide oil (ZnO) against3MTM Cavilon No Sting Barrier FilmTM (NSBF)SYSTEMATIC REVIEWP. Pather et al.(c)2017 Joanna Briggs Institute. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited.(3M Australia, North Ryde, NSW); four studiescompared the effectiveness of Sage Comfort Shield1

3-in-1 wash cloths (Independence Australia Group,Independence, West Footscray, Victoria, Australia)against pH neutral soap and water, 3MTM CavilonNSBF,norinsecleanserandCalmoseptine1(Calmoseptine, Inc., Huntington Beach, CA) barriercream and a standard care regimen, respectively.The study with the standard care regimen did notprovide details of the care regimen. Another studyNumber of records identified through a systematic search Number of additional records identified through other sources e.g. reference list search (N=13)Number of records after duplicates removed (N=1658)excluded (N=175)Number of records screened (N=228)excluded on reading full-text (N=33)Number of full-text articles assessed for eligibility (N=53)Number of articles assessed for quality Number of articles excluded on critical appraisal (N=10)From:  Moher D, Liberati A, Tetzlaff J, Altman DG, The PRISMA Group (2009). Preferred Reporting Items forSystematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses: The PRISMA Statement. PLoS Med 6(6): e1000097.SYSTEMATIC REVIEWP. Pather et al.(c)2017 Joanna Briggs Institute. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited.investigated the effectiveness of a no-rinse cleansingfoam plus moisturizer against a no-rinse pH-bal-anced liquid cleanser and barrier cream.33 Twostudies investigated the effectiveness of foam cleans-ers against soap and water and standard care.19,31Another study evaluated a one-step cleanser protec-tant lotion against wet wipes, dry wipes or washcloths with facility wall dispensed soap34; andanother investigated improvement of skin conditionover time following use of 3MTM Cavilon Dura-bleTM barrier cream (3M Australia, North Ryde,NSW) by comparing skin change over time usinghigh-frequency ultrasound (HFU).32 Description ofstudies including products, comparators and out-comes are provided in Appendix V.

Methodological qualityMethodological quality overall was poor, with fewstudies utilizing randomization, blinding or productallocation concealment. The large variation in com-parators, outcomes, unclear or incomplete data andwide range of measurement tools made it difficult toconduct comparative effectiveness of interventions.Blindingofparticipantsandconsistencyofinterventions were subject to confounding owed tothe nature and form of product application methods.An inability to control for reproducibility of inter-vention protocols due to clinicians' skill or experi-ence,theirpracticevariationsandpossiblesubjectivity of skin assessments were also likelyconfounders. Sample size ranged between 10 and177 participants. Where individual randomizationwas not practical in studies using randomised con-trol trial (RCT) study designs, cluster randomizationby location, admission date and clinical settings wasused.5,19 One study commenced individual random-ization but changed to randomization by settingwithin the care facility due to location of patientsin different areas at different times of the day, forexample bedroom at night and lounge or diningroom during the day.31 Data collected prior tochange in randomization method may have likelycontributed to confounding as no details of a wash-outperiodbetweenproductswasreported.31Reporting of natural attrition and withdrawalsand the inclusion of data on these participants instatistical analysis up to withdrawal were not alwaysclearly discussed31 (see Table 1).Experimental studiesde Baatenburg and Admiraal28YUYUNYUYYYBeeckman et al.15YNUUNYNYYYBohnenkamp et al.29Brunner et al.30YNNYNYNYUYCooper and Gray31UNNUYYYYYYLewis-Byers and Thayer33NNNUNUUYYNPark and Kim19NNNNYYYYYYDescriptive studiesKerr et al.32NYYYNAYNAYYWarshaw et al.34NYYYNANYYYUnable to assess methodological quality as study presented as a poster.

Meta-analysis of the extracted results wasnot possible due to the heterogeneity of the measure-ment tools, outcomes, time points and interventions(Table 2).Four studies (one quasi-experimental, one RCT andtwo pre-post studies) evaluated disposable perinealwash cloths impregnated with a cleanser, moistur-izer and barrier.7,15,29,30 The wash cloths were testedagainst pH neutral soap and water,15 a two-stepStudySkin assessment tool usedde Baatenburg and Admiraal (2004) Zinc Oxideoil vs Cavilon NSBF (No Sting Barrier Film)28Skin condition assessment scale: measured areaand severity of both redness and denudation on a4-point Likert scale 0-3Beeckman et al. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited.cleanser and barrier film spray,30 a no-rinse cleanserand Calmoseptine1 (zinc cream)7 and a standardcare regimen (no details provided)29 (Table 3). Therewas a total of 535 participants across all studies withmajority female participants in Beeckman et al.15 asopposed to Brunner et al.30 where there were moremales and no demographic data on gender providedin the Driver et al. or Bohnenkamp et al. studies.Mean age of participants at entry across the studies,where these data were provided, was between 66.4and 86.3 years. One study was conducted in fournursing homes in Belgium, and the others were set inthe United States of America.15,30 Of these threestudies, two were conducted in intensive care unit(ICU) and one set in acute care that included ICU.All four studies measured IAD prevalence or inci-

dence as an outcome.7,15,29,30 At day 120, the grouptreated with the Sage Comfort Shield1 3-in-1 washcloths had six (8.1%) participants with IAD and thesoap and water group 18 (27.1%).15 The prevalenceof IAD in the Bohnenkamp et al.29 study was nine(81%) and one (9%) in the control/standard caregroup and experimental/wash cloth groups, respect-ively. Brunner et al.30 found 27.3% of participants inthe wipes/washcloth developed IAD as opposed to22.6% of participants treated with the two-stepcleanserth barrier film spray. The forth study byDriver7 yielded a 19% IAD rate in the group treatedwith disposable perineal wash cloths saturated with acleanser, moisturizer and barrier, and a 50% IAD ratein the group treated with a wash cloth and ZnOcream. These findings reveal that use of one-step washcloths with built-in cleanser, moisturizer and barrierhad a lower IAD prevalence and incidence rate whencompared to soap and water or two-step cleanser andbarrier film or zinc oxide cream.The mean IAD severity score (using the IAD SkinCondition Assessment Tool35) was also higher in thesoap and water group (6.4/10) and lower (3.9/10) inthe group treated with Sage Comfort Shield1 3-in-1wash cloths.15 More participants in the 3-in-1 wipesgroup developed moderate skin breakdown (n  1/4 4,12.1%), and the time to skin breakdown was 91.1hours. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited.prevalence was reported between the experimentalgroup (3-in-1 wipes) 8.1% versus control group(soap and water) 27.1% (F  1/4 3.1, P  1/4 0.003).15However, a nonsignificant effect on IAD severity35was reported (experimental: 3.8/10 versus control:6.9/10,F  1/4 0.8,P  1/4 0.06).15Brunneretal.30reported one incident of skin breakdown (3.2%)and 213.3 h to IAD occurrence in the cleanser th bar-barrier film spray (NSBF) group. Average time toskin breakdown was significantly longer in NSBFgroup (n  1/4 6; 213.3 h) versus 3-in-1 wipes group(n  1/4 6; 91.1 h) (F [1, 11]  1/4 5.27, P  1/4 0.045).30 Find-ings from these two studies suggest that 3-in-1 washcloths might have been more effective in preventingIAD incidence and severity than soap and water butmight not have been as effective as an NSBF indelaying skin breakdown.7,15,29,30

Foam cleansersTwo studies (one RCT and one quasi-experimentalstudy) with a total of 163 participants tested the useof foam cleansers in comparison to soap and waterand standard care.19,31 The majority of participantswere female across both studies.19,31 The median agefor both studies at entry to trial was 79 and 85 years,respectively, in the experimental and control groupsin Cooper and Gray31 and 66.2 and 69.7 years inPark and Kim.19 One study was set in aged care inthe United Kingdom, and the second in medical,cardiac, thoracic and neuro ICUs.19,31The groups treated with the foam cleansers had a

largerpercentage of participantswho maintained skinintegrity: 80.4% compared to 37% in the soap andwater group in Cooper and Gray31 Of these twostudies, Cooper and Gray31 also measured skin ero-sion, skin ulceration or skin denudation specific toIAD, and Park and Kim19 measured pressure injury(PI) development creating difficulty in conductingcomparative analysis. In Cooper et al., the proportionof participants who maintained skin integrity was66% in the foam cleanser and 37% in the soap andwater groups, respectively. A moderately higher num-ber of participants (19.6%) had skin redness/eryth-ema or rash in the control (soap and water) group asopposed to 12.2% in the experimental group. Thedifference between the number of participants whodeveloped skin erosion/ulceration or denudation wassmall - two (7.4%): five (10.9%) in the control andthree (7.3%) in the experimental group. Park et al.assessed IAD severity using the Identifying Inconti-nence-Associated Dermatitis and its Severity Instru-ment (IADS)3 between the two groups.19 The IADSinstrument allocates a score ranging from 1 to 3 with atotal of 16 for the anatomical areas with IAD, scoresof 1 or 2 for skin color change and a score of 3 for skinloss. Based on these scores, IAD at its most extensiveand severest will result in an IADS score of 21.3 TheIADS score in the experimental group was 5.19:standard deviation (SD) 3.41 and 14.3 SD 11.7(P value <0.001) in the control group indicating thatthe foam cleanser group had less-severe IAD scoresthan the soap andwater groups. Overall, foam cleans-ers appeared to be slightly more effective than soapand water in preventing or treating IAD or preventingskin breakdown that may lead to pressure ulcers.Results from these studies are detailed in Table 4.InterventionOutcomesResults n (%)Clinisan foam cleanser with dimethicone vs soap and water31Exp. (n  1/4 41)Clinisan

Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited.No-Sting Barrier FilmTwo studies with a total of 106 participantsexamined CavilonTM NSBF as a skin protectant incomparison to ZnO oil (RCT) and Sage ComfortShield1 perineal wash cloths (quasi-experimentalstudy) (Table 5).28,30 It is unclear how participantsin the de Baatenburg and Admiraal28 study wereallocated into the control and experimental groupswith 19 participants in one and 20 in the other as thiscannotbedeterminedbythereportedstudymethods. There were more females included in deBaatenburg and Admiraal,28 and Brunner et al.30had more male participants. Outcomes measured bythese studies differed, making it difficult to pool dataand make comparisons on effectiveness. Participantsacross both studies were between 66.4 and 85.9years.28,30de Baatenburg and Admiraal28 demonstrated thatnine (47.4%) participants treated with ZnO devel-oped skin redness/erythema or a rash as opposed tothree (15%) in those treated with the CavilonTMNSBF.30 The second outcome measured area andseverity of skin erosion, ulceration or denudation.Fewer participants in the CavilonTM NSBF group(n  1/4 3, 16.7%) experienced some degree of skinerosion, ulceration or denudation as opposed tothosetreatedwithZnO,amongwhomeight

(42.9%) developed skin erosion.28 At end of trial,skin integrity deteriorated in two (8.3%) of thosetreated with NSBF as opposed to seven (35.7%) inthose treated with ZnO.28 Total skin damage scoresimproved with both treatments, but were signifi-cantlybetterwith3MTMCavilonTMNSBF(P  1/4 0.04).28 In Brunner et al. (NSBF versus 3-in-1wipes), as discussed previously in comparison toBeeckman et al.,15 more participants in the 3-in-1wipes group developed moderate skin breakdown(n  1/4 4, 12.1%), and the time to skin breakdown was91.1 hours as opposed to one incident of skin break-down (3.2%) and 213.3 hours to skin breakdown inthe cleanser th barrier film spray (3MTM CavilonTMNSBF) group.30 Average time to skin breakdownwas significantly longer in 3MTM CavilonTM NSBFgroup (n  1/4 6; 213.3 h) versus 3-in-1 wipes group(n  1/4 6; 91.1 h) (F [1, 11]  1/4 5.27, P  1/4 0.045).30 Theseresults suggest that 3MTM CavilonTM NSBF wasmore effective in protecting or delaying skin break-down than either ZnO oil and soap and water, andZnO had better skin protectant properties than soapand water.28,30Cont. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited.No-rinse cleanser protectant lotionWarshaw et al., in a prospective quasi-experimentalnon-controlled study, and Lewis-Byers and Thayer,in a prospective descriptive two-group study, testedthe efficacy of a no-rinse cleanser protectant lotionagainst standard care based on soap and waterregimens (Table 6).33,34 Both studies reported dataon a combined total of 50 participants.33,34 War-shaw et al.34 used two separate sites for each inter-vention, and Lewis-Byers and Thayer33 assignedodd- and even-numbered rooms to the two inter-ventions. The Warshaw et al.34 study had more male(73.7%) than female participants (26.3%), andthe gender distribution in the Lewis-Byers andThayer study was not reported.33 The age rangefor all participants across both studies was 41 and105 years.33,34Warshaw et al. reported a decrease in Perineal

Assessment Tool (PAT)10 scores between day oneand day seven from 2.3 to 0.6 in the experimentalgroup with the baseline score reported at 5.9. ThePAT measures risk of IAD on a scale of 1-3 for fouritems including intensity and duration with higherscores indicating more severe IAD. Erythema scoresafter seven days were lower: mean 0.6 and SD 0.8;however, data for PAT scores10 in the control groupwere not provided.34 Lewis-Byers and Thayer33reported that 13 (72.3%) participants in the experi-mental group maintained skin integrity, two (11%)with worsened skin condition and three (16.7%)with improved skin condition. In the control group,nine (69.2%) maintained skin integrity, three (23%)had worsened and one (7.7%) had improved.33 Dataat baseline and completion for the soap and watergroups reported baseline mean skin condition scoresas 0.54; SD 0.88 and at completion mean 0.77; SD1.30. Data for the no-rinse cleanser protectant groupat baseline was mean 0.56; SD 1.15 and at com-pletion mean 0.28; SD 0.83.33 These findings byLewis-Byers and Thayer33 suggest that the no-rinsecleanser lotion was marginally more effective atprotecting the skin from IAD than soap and waterskin care regimens, with only a 3.1% differencebetween the two groups maintaining skin integrity.InrelationtotreatingIAD,Lewis-ByersandThayer33 reported a difference of 9% with improvedskin condition favoring the no-rinse skin liquidcleanser over soap and water.3MTM CavilonTM durable barrier creamIn a prospective cohort descriptive study of 10participants over 14 days, Kerr et al.32 measuredlow echogenic pixel (LEP) imaging of affected skinand compared with the LEP of normal skin adjacentPAT score results(total PAT score  1/4 12)Perineal no-rinse cleanser protec-tant lotion (one step) vs standardcare: wet wipes, dry wipes, or washcloths with soap and water andbarrier cream (two step)34PAT scoreBaseline PAT score 5.9 (12)Exp. no rinse cleanserPAT scores

SYSTEMATIC REVIEWP. Pather et al.(c)2017 Joanna Briggs Institute. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited.to the affected area using HFU (Table 7). The age ofparticipants ranged between 80 and 100 years with60% female participants.32Results showed the mean LEP measure of normalskin for the participants to be 0.123.32 At the firstvisit, the mean LEP baseline measure of affected skinwas 0.345.32 Following treatment with 3MTM Cav-ilonTMdurablebarriercream,themeanLEPmeasures dropped to 0.255 at day seven and0.137 at end of trial on day 14.32 The mean LEPat end of trial dropped to a level close to that ofnormal skin at baseline with a difference of 0.14between the two readings.32 These findings suggestthat 3MTM CavilonTM durable barrier cream waseffective in treating and resolving IAD.32 High-fre-quency ultrasound has been used to in dermatolog-ical practice for histological evaluation of skinlesions and tissue injury and can be used to measurehydration of skin with LEP, which is increased indermal inflammation. It has however not been exten-sively used in clinical trials or practice to diagnoseIAD.36,37 Given the sample size for this study wasvery small, the findings may not be generalizablebased on the fact that HFU and LEP have not beenestablished as reliable measures to detect anddiagnose IAD.DiscussionAt the time of conducting this systematic review,there were a limited number of clinical trials thatcompared products for efficacy in preventing andtreating IAD. The study methodologies varied in theform of RCTs, quasi-experimental, non-controlledand prospective cohort descriptive studies.6,15,19,28-

34 A total of 804 participants were included acrossall 10 studies with the participant numbers rangingbetween 1032 and 3086 in individual studies. This is arelatively small number considering the fact that IADprevalence rates are reported at 50-66% in agedcare and community care residents experiencingincontinence.2,3,38,39It is difficult to fathom the reasons for this lack ofgood-quality published evidence for such widelyused products aiming to treat a common problemin such a large population. This lack of high-qualityexperimental studies is reiterated consistently in theliterature.40 A recent document published from theproceedings of a global IAD expert panel in 2015identifies the comparison of effectiveness of differentskin care products and skin care regimens as an areafor future research on IAD.20 Possible explanationsfor this lack of evidence are difficulty in doubleblinding of participants and care providers due tothe presentation and packaging of products, diffi-culties with controlling individual clinicians' prac-tice, controlling confounders, attitudes and practicesthat may affect results in clinical studies.

Furthermore, to determine whether an exposure isdirectly responsible for the outcome or to test effi-cacy of products that demonstrate a true cause andeffect that is not affected by confounders may bedifficult due to variances/limitations in study popu-lations and nursing practice dissimilarities.15,30,33Confounders such as variances in the length ofexposure to fecal matter following incontinent epi-sodes, amount of friction used to clean skin andremove fecal matter, type of linen and disposablecontainment pads used, amount of skin protectantproducts used and variances in individual nurses'preferences and practices regarding skin care havethe ability to affect results and were acknowledgedas a limitation in some studies and not accounted forin others.30,33 To overcome this, adequate sampleCutaneous hydration of skinwith IAD treated with CavilonDurable barrier cream (3M)application vs healthy adjacentskin32Decreased cutaneous hydrationin skin with IAD as measured bya change in LEP over time todemonstrate improved skin con-dition and resolution of IADusing high-frequency ultrasoundLEP of normal skinusing high-frequencyultrasoundIAD, incontinence-associated dermatitis; LEP, low echogenic pixel; M (SD), mean (standard deviation).SYSTEMATIC REVIEWP. Pather et al.

(c)2017 Joanna Briggs Institute. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited.sizes and randomization should enable researchersto account for these factors and establish a strongerestimate of a causal relationship between skin statusand product use.41Another possible reason may be that IAD is pre-dominately a problem of the aged population thatmay be under-represented or concealed due to thenegative connotations associated with incontinence.The lack of independent funding or the highly com-petitive nature of grant allocations where IADresearch may be deemed less important to othermedical conditions may be another noteworthyreason. Some studies in this review were fundedby pharmacological companies with interests in skinproducts; however, this to a certain degree decreasesconfidence regarding objectivity and introduces con-flict of interest.15,28,34

Aconsistentrecurrentdifficultynotedandacknowledged by studies in this review is the lackof a standardized skin assessment tool to diagnoseand measure IAD. The studies included in this reviewused a wide variety of ways to measure IAD. Table 2provides a list of the skin assessment tools used in thestudies. Seven different skin assessment tools wereused in the eight studies, with the IAD Skin Con-dition and Assessment Tool35 used twice. Infor-mation on validity testing and reliability of skinassessment tools were limited. In addition to thelack of a standardized IAD measurement tool, thefrequent misdiagnosis of IAD as pressure injuriesand vice versa questions the reliability of IAD dataprovided in the studies as no distinction between thetwo conditions were addressed in any of the studiesreviewed. Surprisingly, Cooper and Gray31 used theStirling Pressure Sore Severity Scale. This lack ofconsistent standardized IAD skin assessment reducesobjectivity and opens IAD diagnosis to interpret-ation or misdiagnosis.There is a general overall consensus that skindamage from exposure to urine and or fecal mattercan be reduced by the implementation of structuredskin care regimens that both cleanse and protect skinat risk.20 In a literature review, Beeckman et al.5

reiterated that structured skin care regimens thatincorporate gentle cleansing and the use of skinprotectants were shown to reduce the incidence ofIAD. In the studies reviewed, soap and water regi-mens have shown to be least effective in protectingthe skin against IAD.15,19,31,33,34 No-rinse wipes orcleansers with built-in skin protectants were shownto have some benefit with a positive cost and timesaving effect; however, statistical evidence on effi-cacy on skin protection would be more beneficial forclinicians and healthcare providers.15,19,30,33,34 Inview of the recognition of the positive effect of skinprotectants on treating and preventing IAD, it isimportant to have some collaboration betweenresearchers to encourage studies that exhibit somehomogeneity that will allow statistical meta-analysisto be performed to demonstrate superior effective-ness, if any, between products.Intwoindependentsystematicreviewsoftopical skin care in aged care (one by Hodgkinsonet al. and the other by Beeckman et al.) focusing onIAD as risk factors for PI development, skin carewas identified as a ''major'' issue for nursing.40,42

Park and Kim19 demonstrated that participantswith higher IAD scores or skin damage scores wereat higher risk of developing PU. From the literature,it is clear that IAD as a complication of inconti-nence needs more focus with regard to preventionand treatment. Although research on the conditionhas been initiated, the quality of product effective-ness when reviewed by these two reviews was weakdue to small sample sizes and poor data reporting.Due to similar issues discussed in this review,Hodgkinson et al.40 were unable to make recom-mendations but reiterated that more research intoeffectiveness of topical skin care products wasneeded.ConclusionCurrent research and studies indicate that skin careregimens that include the use of a skin protectantproduct are beneficial in preventing and treatingIAD; however, with current data available, thereis no proven evidence that indicates superior out-comes of any one product. Regardless of heterogen-eity of skin assessment tools, study outcomes andmethodology, most skin care products trialed (withthe exception of soap and water regimens) demon-strated some skin protection qualities. However,more conclusive information on product supremacyis vital. This type of information can be valuable toboth clinicians and healthcare organizations inassisting with the purchase and use of skin careprotectants with both financial and clinical benefits.Hence, more RCTs on product effectiveness onprevention and treatment of IAD are highly recom-mended.SYSTEMATIC REVIEWP. Pather et al.

(c)2017 Joanna Briggs Institute. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited.Implications for practiceThere is insufficient evidence to recommend a singleskin care product for treating or preventing IAD inany care setting as the current evidence lacks rigorand consistency. Outcome measures were nothomogeneous or truly objective, and a standardizedIAD skin assessment tool was deficient acrossall studies.Structured skin care regimens and use of skinproducts do to a certain degree prevent and protectskin against IAD development. The IAD proceedingsfrom an expert panel indicate that dimethicone-con-taining products, acrylate terpolymer products andzinc oxide-based or petrolatum-based products maybe beneficial and recommend them in their ''Cleanse,Protect and Restore'' program as part of interven-tions for prevention and management based on theseverity of IAD.20 However, the wide variety ofproducts available and lack of effectiveness datacreate confusion and have potential cost implica-tions if clinicians are not provided with conclusiveproduct performance power data.

Implications for researchThe use of consistent terminology for IAD asopposed to the wide variety of terms used to labelthe condition will assist research and improve edu-cation of healthcare providers. The testing, triallingand wide use of an industry-approved IAD skinassessment tool is also recommended to provide astandardized tool that can be used across all settingsand by researchers for future studies. This will ensurethe correct diagnosis and treatment for IAD andprevent it being misdiagnosed as a pressure injury.It is also important to consider and include studies inlanguages other than English, and an allocation offunding for publishing in multiple languages andtranslation should be included in research budgetsand proposals. Considering the absence of high-quality true RCTs and the methodology limitationsas evident in the studies included in this review, it isclear that more research comparing effectiveness ofproducts with the measurement of common out-comes that can be statistically measured is required.AcknowledgementsThe authors wish to acknowledge The MaterNursing Research Centre for the support providedin conducting this systematic review.1.
